title: Generics
summary: Write reusable, type-safe functions and classes using TypeScript generics

sections.title: Generics Are Like Adjustable Wrenches
sections.content: A generic is an adjustable wrench — it fits any bolt size, unlike a fixed wrench that only fits one. In TypeScript, generics let you write functions, classes, and interfaces that work with any type while preserving type information. Instead of function identity(value: any): any (loses type info), you write function identity<T>(value: T): T (preserves the exact type). Generics are essential for data structures like arrays, promises, and maps.

sections.title: Generic function and generic constraint
sections.content: Writes a generic function that works with any type and one constrained to objects with a name

sections.title: Generic Constraints with extends
sections.content: The extends keyword in generics restricts what types are allowed. Instead of accepting any type, you can require that T has certain properties. function process<T extends HasId>(item: T) ensures only objects with an id property are passed. This gives you the best of both worlds: flexibility (works with many types) and safety (guarantees required members exist). Common constraints include extends { id: string }, extends Record<string, unknown>, or extends any[].

sections.title: Using any instead of a proper generic
sections.content: The #1 mistake TypeScript developers make is falling back to any when they should use a generic. Wrong: function identity(value: any): any — disables all type checking. Correct: function identity<T>(value: T): T — preserves type information. If you need to access a property, use a constraint: <T extends { length: number }> instead of any. Every any bypass counts as a surrender of type safety.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Write a generic Queue<T> class with enqueue(item: T), dequeue(): T | undefined, and isEmpty(): boolean. The class should work with strings, numbers, or objects. Then create a Queue<number>, add three numbers, dequeue one, and print the remaining two. Add a constraint that T must have a property called priority for a PriorityQueue variant.
hints: What internal data structure (array) should store the queue items?
What is the type of the class field — is it T[] or something else?
How does the extends constraint differ between Queue and PriorityQueue?
